title: Linear Maps, Change of Basis, and Projection

questions.prompt: A linear map $T: V \to W$ has $\dim(\mathcal{N}(T)) = 5$ and $\dim(\mathcal{R}(T)) = 3$. What is $\dim(V)$?
questions.explanation: Rank-nullity applies to the domain: $\dim(V) = \dim(\mathcal{R}(T)) + \dim(\mathcal{N}(T)) = 3 + 5 = 8$. The codimension of $W$ is irrelevant — only the domain dimension is constrained. Note: $\dim(W)$ could be anything $\geq 3$.

questions.prompt: Attention computes $Q = XW_Q$, $K = XW_K$, $V = XW_V$ where $W_Q, W_K, W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$. In linear algebra terms, each projection $W$ is:

options.text: A rotation of $X$ that preserves all inner products
A change-of-basis map projecting $X$ into a $d_k$-dimensional subspace
An orthogonal projection onto the column space of $X$
The inverse of the covariance matrix of $X$

questions.explanation: Each $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d_k}$ is a linear map from $\mathbb{R}^d$ to $\mathbb{R}^{d_k}$, implementing a change of representation. The three heads use different bases ($W_Q$, $W_K$, $W_V$) to project the same input into different subspaces — which is why multi-head attention can capture different relationship types simultaneously.
questions.hint: $W$ maps from $\mathbb{R}^d$ to $\mathbb{R}^{d_k}$. What does such a map do geometrically when $d_k < d$?

questions.prompt: Project vector `v` onto the subspace spanned by the columns of `U`, where `U` has orthonormal columns. The projection matrix is $P = UU^T$.
questions.codeTemplate:
import torch

def project_onto_subspace(U, v):
    # U: (n, k) with orthonormal columns
    # v: (n,)
    return ___BLANK___
questions.explanation: When $U$ has orthonormal columns, $P = UU^T$ is the orthogonal projector onto $\text{col}(U)$. The efficient computation is $U(U^T\vec{v})$: first project $\vec{v}$ onto each basis vector (giving coordinates), then reconstruct. This avoids forming the $n\times n$ matrix $UU^T$ explicitly. This pattern appears in Gram-Schmidt, QR decomposition, and attention.
questions.hint: The projection is $P\vec{v} = UU^T\vec{v}$. Can you factor the computation to avoid building the full $n\times n$ matrix?

questions.prompt: Two $n\times n$ matrices $A$ and $B$ are similar: $B = P^{-1}AP$ for some invertible $P$. Which of the following is the same for $A$ and $B$?

options.text: Their eigenvalues (and hence trace and determinant)

questions.explanation: Similar matrices represent the same linear map in different bases ($P$ is the change-of-basis matrix). The eigenvalues, trace, determinant, rank, characteristic polynomial, and Jordan structure are all basis-independent invariants. Eigenvectors do change (they transform by $P$), and column spaces are entirely different subspaces.
questions.hint: Similar matrices represent the same map seen from different coordinate systems. What properties of a map don't depend on the coordinate system?

questions.prompt: One Gram-Schmidt step: subtract the projection of column `i` of `A` onto the already-orthogonalised column `j` of `Q`. Fill in the projection being subtracted.
questions.explanation: The projection of $\vec{v}$ onto a unit vector $\vec{q}_j$ is $(\vec{q}_j \cdot \vec{v})\vec{q}_j$. Subtracting all such projections removes every component of $A[:,i]$ in the directions already spanned, leaving a vector orthogonal to all previous $Q$ columns. Gram-Schmidt is the algorithm behind QR decomposition and is the heart of the Householder/CGS methods used in practice.
questions.hint: The projection of $\vec{v}$ onto unit vector $\vec{q}$ is $(\vec{q}\cdot\vec{v})\vec{q}$. Here $Q[:,j]$ is already a unit vector.

questions.prompt: Least-squares regression finds $\hat{\vec{w}} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^T\vec{y}$. The fitted values $\hat{\vec{y}} = X\hat{\vec{w}}$ are geometrically:

options.text: The orthogonal projection of $\vec{y}$ onto the column space of $X$
The eigenvector of $X^TX$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue
The vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the same $\ell^2$ norm as $\vec{y}$

questions.explanation: $\hat{\vec{y}} = X(X^TX)^{-1}X^T\vec{y} = P_X\vec{y}$ where $P_X$ is the orthogonal projector onto $\mathcal{C}(X)$. The residual $\vec{y}-\hat{\vec{y}}$ is orthogonal to every column of $X$ (the normal equations say exactly this). Minimising $\|\vec{y}-X\vec{w}\|^2$ is identical to finding the closest point in $\mathcal{C}(X)$ to $\vec{y}$.
questions.hint: Least-squares minimises $\|\vec{y} - X\vec{w}\|^2$. What is the closest point in $\mathcal{C}(X)$ to $\vec{y}$?
